skill.name: microsoft-foundry
skill.description: Guides Microsoft Foundry and Azure AI Foundry workflows for creating projects, provisioning resources, deploying and invoking agents, managing quota, and configuring RBAC.
skill.summary: Manage Azure AI Foundry projects, resources, agents, quotas, and permissions.
skill.author: microsoft

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced a very high score because the skill contains many Markdown command examples, Azure URLs, relative links, and environment variable references. AI review found no prompt injection attempt or clear malicious intent, but the skill intentionally guides cloud provisioning, Docker builds, role assignments, key retrieval, and environment variable handling, so it should publish with operational risk warnings.

medium_findings.title: Cloud Infrastructure Commands Require User Review
medium_findings.description: The skill intentionally guides Azure CLI, Azure Developer CLI, Docker, and ACR operations that can create, modify, deploy, or delete cloud resources. This is legitimate for a Microsoft Foundry operations skill, but the commands can affect billing, access, and production workloads if run without confirmation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are documented workflow steps with clear Azure operational intent, not hidden execution. Risk remains meaningful because they can provision, deploy, delete, or change paid cloud resources.

medium_findings.title: Environment Variables And Secrets Are Handled During Deployment
medium_findings.description: The deployment workflow scans source code for environment variables, pre-fills values from azd configuration, creates local environment files, and includes environment variables in hosted agent payloads. Sensitive values are expected to be masked, but mishandling could expose credentials or long-lived configuration secrets.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The context confirms legitimate deployment configuration handling and explicit masking guidance. The risk is not malicious, but credentials and environment values are part of the workflow.

medium_findings.title: RBAC And Service Principal Workflows Can Create Privileged Credentials
medium_findings.description: The RBAC workflow includes role assignment, managed identity configuration, and service principal creation for CI/CD. These actions are legitimate, but generated credentials and broad roles require least-privilege review and secure storage outside chat history.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The workflow clearly describes identity and permission management, including service principal setup. The concern is operational exposure from credential creation and role assignment rather than malicious behavior.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer Matched Documentation Syntax As Dangerous Code
low_findings.description: Many Ruby backtick, weak cryptography, and command substitution findings are false positives caused by Markdown inline code, code fences, headings, or normal shell examples. No evidence found that the skill hides executable code or performs automatic command execution by itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sampled locations are Markdown instructions and examples, not source code loaded by the skill runtime. The pattern labels do not match the semantic context.

low_findings.title: Official Documentation And Azure API URLs Are Hardcoded
low_findings.description: The network findings are primarily links to Microsoft documentation, Azure portal pages, GitHub samples, and Azure management endpoints used for quota checks. These URLs support the skill purpose and do not indicate data exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed URLs are expected Azure and Microsoft references or management APIs. I did not find evidence of suspicious third-party collection endpoints.

low_findings.title: Relative Links Triggered Path Traversal Heuristics
low_findings.description: The filesystem path traversal findings appear to come from relative Markdown links between skill documents. These links help route readers to related workflow files and do not access arbitrary filesystem paths.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence points to documentation-relative navigation rather than filesystem reads or writes. The scanner found traversal tokens, but not an exploit path.

dangerous_patterns.title: External CLI And Container Build Instructions
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill contains command examples for az, azd, Docker, and ACR. These are expected for the workflow, but users should review commands before execution and confirm target subscriptions, resource groups, and registries.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is confirmed and legitimate for Azure operations. The main risk is accidental or over-permissive execution in a live cloud environment.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential And Environment Configuration Handling
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill handles environment values, keys, and service principal output as part of Foundry setup. Treat generated values as sensitive and avoid exposing them in logs or transcripts.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The content explicitly covers keys, environment variables, and service principal credentials. This is necessary for the skill but needs careful handling.

content.value_statement: Microsoft Foundry setup spans projects, resources, agents, quotas, and permissions. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code structured workflows for Azure AI Foundry operations.
actual_capabilities: Guides Azure AI Foundry project creation with Azure Developer CLI workflows.
Documents Foundry resource provisioning with Azure CLI and provider registration steps.
Supports creating, deploying, invoking, cloning, and troubleshooting Foundry agents.
Provides quota and capacity workflows for regional TPM, PTU, and deployment planning.
Explains RBAC role assignment, managed identity setup, and service principal configuration.
References Microsoft Agent Framework patterns for Python agents and multi-agent workflows.
limitations: Requires valid Azure credentials, permissions, and installed CLI tools for many workflows.
Does not replace Azure cost, security, or production change-management review.
Some model, quota, and regional availability details can change outside the skill files.
The skill provides guidance and MCP tool routing; it does not guarantee cloud operation success.

use_cases.title: Set up a Foundry project
use_cases.description: Create a new Azure AI Foundry project with supporting infrastructure, then capture endpoint and resource details for agent work.

use_cases.title: Deploy and test agents
use_cases.description: Package hosted agents, deploy prompt or container agents, start containers, and invoke agents for validation.

use_cases.title: Manage access and quota
use_cases.description: Audit RBAC assignments, configure identities, check regional quota, and plan capacity for production deployments.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Foundry project
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Microsoft Foundry skill to help me create a new Azure AI Foundry project. Ask for missing project name, subscription, and region details before running commands.

prompt_templates.title: Deploy a hosted agent
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Microsoft Foundry deploy workflow for my existing agent project. Detect the language, identify required environment variables, and confirm values before deployment.

prompt_templates.title: Diagnose quota limits
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Microsoft Foundry quota workflow to check regional quota for a GPT-4o deployment and recommend regions with available capacity.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare CI/CD access
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Microsoft Foundry RBAC workflow to design least-privilege service principal access for deploying agents from CI/CD.

output_examples.input: Help me create a new Foundry project for agent development.
output_examples.output: The assistant checks Azure CLI and azd prerequisites, asks for project name and region, initializes the Foundry starter template, provisions infrastructure, and reports the project endpoint.

output_examples.input: Deploy this Python agent to Azure AI Foundry.
output_examples.output: The assistant detects the Python project, scans required environment variables, proposes a Docker build path, confirms registry and agent settings, and validates the running agent.

output_examples.input: Why did my model deployment fail with QuotaExceeded?
output_examples.output: The assistant checks regional quota, compares available capacity, suggests deleting unused deployments or selecting another region, and explains when to request more quota.

best_practices: Confirm subscription, resource group, region, and cost impact before running provisioning or deletion commands.
Mask secrets and store service principal credentials in a secure vault or CI/CD secret store.
Use least-privilege RBAC roles and validate assignments at the narrowest practical scope.
anti_patterns: Do not run non-interactive deployment workflows without supplying and reviewing all required values first.
Do not paste long-lived secrets or service principal passwords into shared chat transcripts.
Do not use this skill for unrelated Azure Functions, App Service, or generic Azure resource workflows.

faq.question: What does this skill help automate?
faq.answer: It guides Azure AI Foundry project setup, resource creation, agent deployment, invocation, troubleshooting, quota checks, and RBAC management.

faq.question: Does it require Azure CLI access?
faq.answer: Many workflows require Azure CLI, Azure Developer CLI, Docker, or Foundry MCP tools, depending on the task.

faq.question: Can it deploy hosted agents?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes workflows for containerizing hosted agents, building images, updating agent definitions, starting containers, and testing responses.

faq.question: Can it help with quota issues?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains regional quota checks, PTU planning, common quota errors, and options for freeing or requesting capacity.

faq.question: Is it safe to use with secrets?
faq.answer: Use caution. The skill handles environment variables, keys, and service principal output, so sensitive values should be masked and stored securely.

faq.question: Why is the audit risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill is not malicious, but it guides real cloud commands, identity changes, and credential handling that can affect security and billing.
